Technical Sergeant Leonard Matlovich. It does not bear his name because he intended to be a monument to all gay veterans
Very interesting man. Worth going down a rabbit hole over. Here's a start: https://time.com/4019076/40-years-leonard-matlovich/

item.title: What Happened Today #116
item.description: ? The center cannot hold when power attacks its own foundations. Trump pardons corrupt sheriffs while calling the Justice Department "corrupt," attacks education at every level from Harvard to NIH research funding, and uses Memorial Day messages to attack judges blocking his deportation orders. But resistance is everywhere: dozens of NIH scientists walk out after their new director tells them they funded studies that led to the pandemic, environmental groups file lawsuits against illegal executive orders, and federal judges keep ruling against lawless overreach. The question isn't whether democracy can survive this assault, but it's whether we'll fight hard enough to make sure it does. Meanwhile, don't rely on the algorithm. Let me send this free and private summary to you every night so that you never miss what's going on. Add your email on the homepage of GovBrief.today #GovBriefToday #Resist 1. https://go.govbrief.today/witkoff-gaza-negotiations 2. https://go.govbrief.today/deportees-djibouti-murphy 3. https://go.govbrief.today/volvo-cut-3k 4. https://go.govbrief.today/nih-staff-walkout 5. https://go.govbrief.today/harvard-funds-trade-schools 6. https://go.govbrief.today/fbi-invesigating-past-cases 7. https://go.govbrief.today/trump-pardons-jenkins 8. https://go.govbrief.today/memorial-day-message YESTERDAY - https://go.govbrief.today/internior-negative-america FIGHTING BACK - https://go.govbrief.today/earthjustice-sues-fishing
item.dc:creator: GeorgeBounacos

item.title: This Memorial Day: Major Dick Bong
item.description:
Major Richard "Dick" Bong (born September 20th, 1920 - died August 6th, 1945) was a U.S. Army Air Force pilot credited with being the highest "scoring" fighter ace in American history. In 1938, while attending university with the goal of becoming a school teacher, he began taking flight lessons and would join the Army's Aviation Cadet Program in mid-1941. He was commissioned as an officer and pilot in January 1942, just two months after the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor and the United States' subsequent entry into World War II.
While training to fly his signature aircraft, the Lockheed P-38 Lightning, in California shortly after his commissioning, he "buzzed" (meaning he flew incredibly close to the ground) the house of a fellow pilot to celebrate his recent marriage, and then buzzed San Francisco's busy Market Street. This of course got him reprimanded by his commanding officer, General George Kenney, commanding officer of the Army's Fourth Air Force. However, Kenney also directly told him "If you didn't want to fly down Market Street, I wouldn't have you in my Air Force, but you are not to do it any more and I mean what I say." As punishment for his "crimes", he was ordered to wash an Oakland woman's laundry as his low flying had blown all of her laundry off of her drying clothesline. His greater punishment though, was being grounded as the rest of his unit was deployed to the United Kingdom, leaving him behind in the states.
Above: another P-38 Lightning, not Bong's aircraft. Following his grounding, Bong was instead deployed to the Pacific theater with another group of P-38 pilots. He scored his first aerial victories on December 27th, 1942 when he shot down two Japanese fighters, one of them being the infamous A6M "Zero", over New Guinea, earning him his first of two Silver Stars (the third highest award he could receive). Just two weeks later, on January 8th, 1943, Bong claimed his 5th aerial kill which would make him a "fighter ace". By the end of World War II, only 1,300 American pilots could claim to be aces. When Bong was promoted to Captain in August of 1943, he had claimed 15 confirmed kills and would claim 5 more by the end of 1943, all of which were in the vicinity of Papua New Guinea. Bong was awarded the Army's Distinguished Service Cross, the second highest award a U.S. servicemember could receive. As a side note, Bong considered his gunnery skills to be poor, so his signature tactic was to use his P-38 Lightning's twin turbosupercharged Allison V12 engines to close the distance between him and his target (the P-38 was the fastest production aircraft ever developed until the advent of jet engines). At close range, he would unleash the P-38's armament, consisting of four .50 caliber machine guns and a 20mm cannon, with devastating results. The P-38 is just a fascinatingly unique plane that I'll likely do a write up on at some point.
Above: footage of the P-38 Lightning, again not Bong himself. In April of 1944, he surpassed Major Eddie Rickenbacker's 26 aerial victories, who was the highest scoring U.S. fighter ace of World War I. This achievement got him sent on a protracted war bond promotion tour across the U.S., before returning to the Pacific in September. As a major, Bong was capable of commanding a squadron (16-24 P-38s for the USAAF in WWII), but instead chose to remain as a flight (4 P-38s). In addition to being allowed to continue flying missions, he was given explicit instructions to "avoid seeking combat" which he did not follow. Bong's continued status as a combat pilot was very unusual; U.S. doctrine at the time practically required their best pilots to essentially "retire" from combat and serve as instructors and other supporting roles. In my opinion, it's likely General Kenney and his other commanders' high opinion of him "allowed" him this special status. Bong did not shirk his instructor duties either, he continued to instruct and mentor rookies in theater as an advanced gunnery instructor and often flying with rookies in his flight or element. While on its face this may sound strange, removing veteran pilots from combat was a key factor in the U.S. dominating in the air war over the Pacific as rookie American pilots were shipping out for their first combat tours with real skills and tactics learned from actual experience flying the same planes and fighting the same enemies. The Japanese on the other hand practiced the exact opposite, keeping their best on the front lines which inevitably resulted in their eventual deaths and no lessons learned for new Japanese pilots. With the increased intensity in combat as the war went on, the number of veteran Japanese pilots quickly dropped and the rookies were left to fend for their own against their vastly better trained American counterparts.
Above: Bong in front of his P-38J, showing 25 kills painted on the side of the cockpit. From October to December of 1944, he flew missions over the Philippines, scoring another 12 confirmed kills and bringing the total to 40: the highest of any U.S. pilot yet. He was also awarded a Congressional Medal of Honor, the highest award any U.S. servicemember can receive, making him one of just 471 Americans recipients for their actions in the Second World War. At the end of 1944, Bong's status as the "ace of aces" (the highest scoring ace) meant that his combat days were officially over as higher-ups would not let a pilot of his stature be killed in action due to his value for morale and propaganda.
Above: a typical Lockheed P-80 Shooting Star jet fighter, not Bong's plane. Unfortunately, Major Bong's story does not have a happy end. After his retirement from combat, he became a test pilot for the Army Air Force and would perform acceptance flights for new aircraft before the Army would accept them from the manufacturer. On his 12th acceptance flight for Lockheed P-80 Shooting Stars, the first U.S. jet fighter plane, he was killed when the P-80's fuel pump failed during takeoff. Though he ejected from the plane (the P-80 was the first U.S. fighter to be equipped with ejection seats), the plane's altitude was too low and the ejection seat's parachute did not have time to open. He was killed when his ejection seat struck the ground. The fatal crash occurred on August 6th, 1945: the day the United States dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima; just nine days before Japanese Emperor Hirohito announced their surrender. Bong was only 24 years old at the time of his death. Just 6 months earlier, he had married Marjorie Vattendahl, whom he had met while on leave in his home state of Wisconsin at the end of 1943.
Bong and Vattendahl in his P-38 prior to their marriage.
Major Bong after renaming his P-38J Lightning "Marge" and adorning it with photograph of Marjorie. Bong's nose art featuring Marjorie was unusual as most USAAF nose art in World War II featured paintings of pinup girls, animals, etc., rather than a classy photo of a girlfriend.
Major Richard Bong is interred at the Poplar Cemetery in Poplar, Wisconsin (his hometown). Marjorie was buried next to him following her passing in 2003. I tried to find a better quality image, but was unable. Yes this post started because he has an amazing name; I still want everyone to remember this incredible man for Memorial Day. If you choose to visit his gravesite, please remember not to attempt to clean it to avoid damaging his grave markers. Leaving flowers, flags, and other appropriate items is fine as far as I am aware.

item.title: Red Sonja (2025)
item.description:
The She-Devil with a sword will return to the silver screen late this year.
Italian actress Matilda Lutz (Rings, Revenge, Medici - The Magnificent) is Red Sonja,
British actress Wallis Day (Krypton, Infinite, Batwoman) is Dark Annisia,
and it is directed by M. J. Bassett (Solomon Kane, Ash vs Evil Dead, Reacher).
The 1985 film with Brigitte Nielsen was [loosely] based on the Marvel Comics series,
while this is [loosely?] based on the Dynamite Entertainment series by Gail Simone
and, instead of PG-13 as the 1985 film, will be R-rated
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: 1:1400 Enterprise-D. I worked on this thing on-and-off for around a year. I made a lot of mistakes, some of which I fixed, and others I couldn't.I wasn't going for screen accuracy, I just wanted it to look good. The pink hull panels were an accident, I a
